1: The divine warrior theme: YHWH delivers Og of Bashan into Israel's hands as he delivered Sihon. This pattern of God fighting for his people — not Israel's military prowess winning the day — is the Old Testament's recurring testimony to a God who acts in history on behalf of those he has chosen. The NT presents Christ as the ultimate divine warrior who defeats the last and greatest enemies: sin, death, and the powers (1 Corinthians 15:24–26; Colossians 2:15).
2: The "do not fear" command paired with divine assurance — "I have given him into your hand" — is the covenant pattern of promised victory before the battle is fought. This proleptic assurance mirrors the NT structure where Christ's victory over death is declared before the believer's own death is faced (John 16:33: "In the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world"). The same God who assured Moses assures those united to Christ.
3: YHWH gives Og into Israel's hands — the complete subjugation of a hostile king with no survivors. As a revelation of God's faithfulness: what he promises, he delivers. The New Testament presents Christ as the one in whom all of God's promises are "yes and amen" (2 Corinthians 1:20) — every word of assurance spoken in the Old Testament finds its fulfillment in him.
4: Sixty fortified cities taken — the scope of the victory exceeds any natural expectation. The comprehensive nature of the divine warrior's triumph foreshadows the unlimited scope of Christ's victory: "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me" (Matthew 28:18). No fortress of human power or spiritual opposition can withstand the one to whom ultimate authority belongs.
5: High walls, gates, and bars — the most formidable military defenses of the ancient world — fall before YHWH's people. The prophetic tradition picks up this imagery: "I will break in pieces the gates of bronze and cut through the bars of iron" (Isaiah 45:2). Christ extends this victory to the gates of Hades itself: "the gates of hell shall not prevail against it" (Matthew 16:18). A theme running from this verse through to the unlocking of every stronghold in Christ.
6: The herem (devotion to destruction) applied to Og's cities — the most theologically difficult aspect of the conquest narratives. The herem reveals the holiness of God's judgment against Canaanite evil accumulated over four centuries (Genesis 15:16). The New Testament does not abolish divine judgment but relocates its center: at the cross, Christ bears the judgment himself (2 Corinthians 5:21; Galatians 3:13), exhausting its claim. Revelation 19 restores the divine-warrior imagery for the final judgment, but the cross stands between the conquest herem and the eschatological herem as the place where judgment was absorbed.
7: Livestock and plunder kept — in contrast to the full herem applied to persons. The distinction between what is consecrated to destruction and what is legitimately claimed as provision reflects the covenant community's practical sustenance. As a revelation of God's character: he provides for his people's needs even within the severity of holy war, a theme the NT develops in Christ as the one who provides abundantly for those who follow him (John 10:10).
8: The territory east of the Jordan taken from the Amorite kings — the first installment of the promised land. The land as YHWH's gift, received through combat, is a type of the inheritance believers receive in Christ: not earned by their own strength but received through the victory of their representative head. Ephesians 1:11 speaks of believers as having "obtained an inheritance" in Christ — the vocabulary of naḥălâ translated into the NT register.
9: Parenthetical: Hermon's alternate names among the Sidonians (Sirion) and Amorites (Senir). An administrative note preserving the geographic memory of the conquest. Even in passing details, the narrative bears witness that YHWH's sovereignty extends over terrain known by the names of pagan peoples — the whole earth belongs to him (Psalms 24:1), a confession the NT expresses as Christ's universal lordship (Colossians 1:16–17).
10: The full extent of the territory from the plateau cities through Gilead and all Bashan — the comprehensive claim of the promise being fulfilled. The boundary of the inheritance describes what God had committed to give. Every territorial detail in the conquest narrative testifies to God's precision in keeping covenant promises — a precision the NT claims is displayed ultimately in Christ, in whom every promise of God is yes (2 Corinthians 1:20).
11: Og king of Bashan — the last of the Rephaim, legendary for his enormous size (his iron bed measured nine cubits by four). The defeat of the Rephaim, the giant remnant associated with primordial opposition to God's people, carries symbolic weight: God's people conquer even the seemingly unconquerable. As a shadow: Christ's defeat of death, "the last enemy" (1 Corinthians 15:26), fulfills what these giant-slaying episodes anticipate — the ultimate enemy no human power could overcome has been destroyed by God's representative champion.
12: The Reubenite and Gadite allotment — Moses begins distributing the Transjordanian land as covenant-promised inheritance. As a theme: the God who promises is the God who distributes. The tribal allotments of Deuteronomy and Joshua prefigure the spiritual inheritance distributed to all who are heirs through Christ (Galatians 3:29; Romans 8:17 — "fellow heirs with Christ").
13: Bashan given to the half-tribe of Manasseh — part of the Joseph inheritance, the tribe of the patriarch whose life was shaped by unexpected preservation and redemption from suffering to exaltation. The Manassite allotment in the land captured from Og carries the Joseph typology forward. Christ as the ultimate Joseph figure — betrayed, executed, raised to lordship — ultimately distributes the inheritance he himself secured.
14: Jair the Manassite names the conquered villages after himself — Havvoth-jair. Naming the land enacts ownership; it is the covenantal naming that says "this belongs to us." In the new creation, the promised inheritance is one where Christ's name is written on those who receive it (Revelation 2:17; Deuteronomy 3:12) — the ownership enacted through a new name rather than a place name.
15: "To Machir I gave Gilead." A brief note establishing inheritance. The covenant God is a distributing God — he gives territory, inheritance, possession. This giving character of YHWH is revealed fully in Christ: "For God so loved the world that he gave" (John 3:16). The greatest giving is not land but the Son himself, through whom all true inheritance is received.
16: The territorial boundaries described precisely — the Arnon Valley, the Jabbok River, the Ammonite border. The precision of the covenant inheritance is a revelation of God's character: he does not give vaguely but specifically. In Christ, the inheritance is equally precise: resurrection life, adoption as sons, the Holy Spirit as guarantee (Ephesians 1:13–14). The specificity of the promise is a mark of its trustworthiness.
17: The Arabah and the Jordan as western boundary; the Sea of the Arabah under the slopes of Pisgah. Geographic exactitude in the narrative — the boundary is real territory, not symbolic space. The concreteness of the promised land is the OT foundation for the NT's equally concrete promises: a bodily resurrection, a renewed earth (Revelation 21:1), an inheritance that is "imperishable, undefiled, and unfading" (1 Peter 1:4). The promises are real precisely because YHWH's covenant has always been embodied and territorial.
18: "The LORD your God has given you this land to possess" — the east-Jordan tribes receive the land but are called to fight for their brothers until everyone has rest. The covenant call to solidarity — those who have received must serve those still waiting to receive — is a theme the NT extends to the whole people of God. "Bear one another's burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ" (Galatians 6:2). Rest is received together, not hoarded privately.
19: Wives, children, and livestock remain behind — the fighting men cross armed. The separation of those who go forward to battle from those who remain in safety reflects the covenant structure: the representative bears the burden so others can be protected. Christ as the one who goes before his people — "I go to prepare a place for you" (John 14:2) — carries this representative-advance pattern to its ultimate expression.
20: "Until the LORD gives rest to your fellow Israelites as he has given rest to you." The concept of rest (mĕnûḥâ) is theologically central. Hebrews 3–4 develops the "Sabbath rest" of God as the eschatological inheritance into which Christ leads his people — the rest that Joshua could not fully give (Hebrews 4:8) because it pointed forward to a greater rest. The Transjordanian tribes who already have rest are the pattern; all Israel entering rest is the type; Christ's people entering the eternal Sabbath is the fulfillment.
21: "Your eyes have seen everything the LORD your God has done to these two kings. The LORD will do the same to all the kingdoms you are about to enter." Moses commissions Joshua on the basis of what has already been seen — past faithfulness as the ground for future confidence. In Christ, the resurrection is the already-accomplished act that grounds all future confidence: the one who conquered death will bring his people through every remaining obstacle (1 Corinthians 15:20–22).
22: "Do not fear them, for the LORD your God himself fights for you." This verse is Moses's commissioning word to Joshua, who will face Canaan. It is the assurance that undergirds Joshua's entire career — and it names Joshua (Yēhôšûaʿ = "YHWH saves") as the vessel of divine warfare. The name is not incidental: the NT uses the Greek form of this name (Iēsous = Jesus) to identify the one in whom "YHWH saves" is ultimately and perfectly true (Matthew 1:21). Joshua types Christ both in name and in this commission to lead the people through fear into rest.
23: Moses pleads with YHWH: "I pleaded with the LORD at that time." The prayer of Moses at this point reveals the depth of his longing to enter the land — the goal of forty years of leading. That Moses, the great intercessor and mediator of the covenant, is refused entry reveals that no human mediator, however faithful, can bring the people into the fullness of the promise. Only the greater Mediator (1 Timothy 2:5; Hebrews 8:6) can do that.
24: "O Lord GOD, you have only begun to show your servant your greatness and your mighty hand. What god is there in heaven or on earth who can perform deeds and mighty acts like yours?" The incomparability of YHWH — no god in heaven or on earth can match his deeds. This is the exclusive monotheism that the NT expresses Christologically: the incomparable God has acted incomparably in the resurrection of Jesus (Ephesians 1:19–21 — "the immeasurable greatness of his power... that he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead").
25: Moses asks to cross over and see "the good land beyond the Jordan — that good hill country and Lebanon." The longing for the promised land is among the most poignant moments in all of Scripture. The promised land, with its milk and honey and good hill country, is the concrete shape of covenant blessing. Hebrews 11:16 reads the patriarchal longing for "a better country, that is, a heavenly one" — what Moses sees from Pisgah becomes a shadow of the better country that all of God's people ultimately seek.
26: "The LORD was angry with me on your account" — Moses bears the consequence of Israel's rebellion (see Deuteronomy 1:37; Numbers 20:12). Moses is excluded from the inheritance he spent his life moving toward, and this exclusion is explicitly connected to Israel's sin. This is a shadow of substitutionary consequence: the mediator bears what the people deserved. The contrast with Christ is equally important — Moses bore the consequence (exclusion) without removing the guilt; Christ bore the ultimate consequence (death) and actually removed the sin (Hebrews 9:26–28).
27: "Go up to the top of Pisgah and look... for you shall not cross this Jordan." Moses sees the promised land from afar but does not enter. Hebrews 11:13 speaks of the patriarchs who "died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar." Moses sees what he cannot yet receive — a vision of the promise without its possession. In Christ, what is seen from afar is grasped and entered: "the Word became flesh and dwelt among us" (John 1:14) is the promised land becoming present.
28: "Commission Joshua, and encourage him and strengthen him, for he shall cross over at the head of this people and shall lead them to inherit the land you will see." Joshua is formally commissioned to succeed Moses as the one who will lead Israel into the inheritance. Joshua as type of Christ: he crosses the Jordan (the boundary between wilderness and rest), leads the people into what Moses could not give them, and distributes the inheritance. Hebrews 4:8 makes this typology explicit: "if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on" — pointing to Jesus, the greater Joshua, who gives the true Sabbath rest.
29: Israel camps at Beth-peor — the location associated with the Baal Peor apostasy (Num 25; Deuteronomy 4:3). Ending the Transjordanian conquest narrative here is not incidental; the narrative places the community at the very site of their greatest recent failure as they prepare to receive Moses's great covenantal address. As a revelation of God's mercy: YHWH continues to dwell with and lead his people even in the shadow of their failure. This mercy is the character of God the NT declares fully revealed in Christ (John 1:16–17 — "grace and truth came through Jesus Christ").
